Binoy, Badal and Dinesh

[Azaadi](https://sadashree.substack.com/s/azaadi/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# Binoy, Badal and Dinesh

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Aug 20, 2024

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

1

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!REC0!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F3b495dda-0138-45f2-b3a9-92fcb065fa43_602x330.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!REC0!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F3b495dda-0138-45f2-b3a9-92fcb065fa43_602x330.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Writers Building in Kolkata, designed by Thomas Lyon in 1777, was so called after the junior clerks of the East India Company, called writers who worked here. It was the HQ of the East India Company and later the British Raj over Bengal Presidency. During the freedom struggle, the building was witness to one of the more high-profile assassinations of a senior British official, by three young men, all of whom belonged to the Bengal Volunteers Group.

The Bengal Volunteers embodied that fierce, morally charged patriotism that was ready to defy not only colonial rule but even the cautious boundaries of mainstream politics. Their formation during the 1928 Kolkata session of the Indian National Congress,under Subhash Chandra Bose’s vision,speaks volumes about the ideological ferment of that moment.

And with Major Satya Gupta guiding their evolution, the group channelled youthful idealism into a disciplined, coordinated insurgency.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!wgCo!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F02f5484d-44d2-4043-9b41-6af616fbe141_602x899.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!wgCo!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F02f5484d-44d2-4043-9b41-6af616fbe141_602x899.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Operation Freedom wasn’t just an act of retaliation; it was the outcome of many factors. Police repression had grown brutal and indiscriminate, targeting anyone showing even mild dissent. The Volunteers responded not with mass protests or resolutions, but with targeted action, reshaping fear into agency. Their strategy borrowed from military discipline and revolutionary idealism, bridging martial precision with patriotic sacrifice.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!vRtO!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F4c21fc03-a6e1-415e-b478-72188938af34_330x422.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!vRtO!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F4c21fc03-a6e1-415e-b478-72188938af34_330x422.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Benoy Krishna Basu born on September 11, 1908 in a small village in Munshiganj district, now part of Bangladesh. He wasn’t just shaped by historical currents; he chose to shape them, with precision and conviction.

The journey from Munshiganj to Dhaka was one from innocence to awakening, where his entry into Sir Salimullah Medical College became both an academic and ideological pivot. Hemachandra Ghosh’s imprint through the Mukhti Sangha is subtle but powerful, passing the revolutionary dharma from one student to another.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!02tX!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F507661a0-226b-460b-9436-079448c8c6b7_300x406.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!02tX!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F507661a0-226b-460b-9436-079448c8c6b7_300x406.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Badal Gupta was born in a small village now located in Bikrampur region of Bangladesh, and influenced by the revolutionary activities of his paternal uncles Dharani Nath Gupta and Nagendra Nath Gupta, who were involved in the Alipore bomb case, joined the Bengal Volunteers in 1928.

He wasn’t simply reacting to British atrocities, he was answering a call etched into his family’s history, moulded by the defiance of his uncles.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!aOWP!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F3ed43fe1-f795-4f42-892e-5cef57d0b138_330x440.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!aOWP!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F3ed43fe1-f795-4f42-892e-5cef57d0b138_330x440.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Dinesh Gupta was born on December 6, 1911 in Josholong, now in Murshiganj district of Bangladesh. He joined the Bengal Volunteers while studying in Dhaka, and for some time he was in Midnapore, training other revolutionaries in usage of bombs and ammunition.

His training role in Midnapore adds a strategic edge to the narrative—he wasn’t just a participant but a mentor of resistance, shaping others through practical knowledge of weapons and sabotage.

And in Dhaka, amidst the intellectual ferment, Dinesh discovers not just patriotism but a disciplined method—no fiery slogans, just cold resolve.

Their target was the Inspector General of Police, Col N.S.Simpson, and it was with good reason, a a stand against systemic cruelty. His treatment of political prisoners embodied everything the revolutionaries detested about colonial power.

Earlier in August 1933, the trio had shot dead Lowman, the police Inspector general at Dhaka, and these were not just impulsive attacks, they were part of a larger strategy against state sponsored oppression.

And why Writers Building? Because it’s corridors echoed with imperial decrees. And it’s there they chose to disrupt that power narrative.

December 8, 1930 wasn’t just a day—it became a rupture in the imperial narrative, orchestrated with cinematic precision. The disguise in Western clothing wasn’t merely tactical; it was symbolic. They used the colonizer’s own cultural façade to slip past the gates of power—and then shattered its spine.

Simpson wasn’t just a man to them—he was the embodiment of cruelty, and they delivered retribution with surgical finality.

Twynam, Prentice, and Nelson caught in the crossfire weren’t collateral; they were part of a system that had long inflicted pain. The bullets weren’t just physical—they were ideological.

Writers’ Building turned into a crucible where three young minds clashed with empire—not with slogans, but with action born of moral outrage.

Though the trio were overpowered, they refused to surrender. Badal took cyanide, not to just escape, it was his rejection of the British legal system. He would not allow them the spectacle of his trial.

Benoy and Dinesh, shot themselves, as they chose autonomy over imprisonment, knowing full well the myth they were crafting in blood. Dinesh’s survival and execution added a haunting coda—he became a living witness to their pact, and eventually the last offering on the altar of freedom.

Dalhousie Square—once a symbol of colonial power—is today BBD Bagh, echoing the names of *Benoy*, *Badal*, and *Dinesh*. Their initials, etched into the city’s very cartography, remind Kolkata to never forget the price of its freedom.

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

1

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)
